symbols: angel kneeling before Mary — the heavenly messenger delivering God's invitation
dove descending from above — the Holy Spirit overshadowing Mary
veil or curtain between them — the threshold between heaven and earth about to be crossed
Mary's gesture of acceptance — the fiat, 'let it be done according to your word'
interior domestic setting — the Incarnation entering ordinary human life
description: An arched panel painting showing the Annunciation scene in a domestic interior. The Angel Gabriel kneels at left with white wings, one hand raised in greeting, wearing light-colored robes. The Virgin Mary stands at right in a flowing gown, her body slightly turned toward the angel with a gesture of humble acceptance. Between them, a sheer curtain or veil hangs from the ceiling. A dove descends from the upper portion. A small vase with a lily or flower is visible. The palette is muted with soft grays, whites, and warm earth tones.
theological_description: The Annunciation (LUK.1.26-38) marks the decisive moment when eternity enters time and God becomes man. Gabriel's message to Mary — 'you will conceive in your womb and bear a son, and you shall call his name Jesus' (LUK.1.31) — announces the fulfillment of Isaiah's prophecy: 'Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel' (ISA.7.14). The dove descending represents the Holy Spirit's overshadowing (LUK.1.35), the creative power of God bringing about a new creation within Mary's womb. The domestic setting emphasizes that the most cosmic event in history occurs in the most ordinary of places — a truth that permeates the Gospel. Mary's response — 'Behold, I am the servant of the Lord; let it be to me according to your word' (LUK.1.38) — is the perfect human response to divine initiative, reversing Eve's disobedience with faithful obedience.

scripture_refs.reason: The Annunciation, the direct subject of this panel

The virgin shall conceive, the prophecy fulfilled at this moment
